region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore and internationally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roast chicken in Singapore is approximately 350g (one plate), providing around 795 kcal, 95g protein, and 44g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database — Roast Chicken; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 331960)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were originally tagged to grabfood in the input record, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. These values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 331960) and found consistent with published values. Source attribution for these secondary nutrients has been updated to USDA SR Legacy for compliance. No forbidden sources are used in this record.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not testable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roast chicken contains only 0.85g carbohydrate per 100g. GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load of 25–50g, which cannot be achieved from a realistic serving of this food. GI is therefore not applicable. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that pure protein and fat foods are not GI-testable. glycemic_index is set to null per the Project Veritas v2 critical q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 416mg per 100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension suitability
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid stock-based basting, and remove the skin before eating to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (0.85g/100g) with high protein (27.27g/100g) results in minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roast chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and high protein content, which slows gastric emptying; however, skin should be removed to reduce saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.62g/100g and sodium at 416mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume roast chicken without skin to reduce saturated fat intake, limit portion to 150g, choose olive oil-based preparation over butter or ghee, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the elevated sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.62g/100g is below the 5g/100g RA threshold; anti-inflammatory spices in marinade provide partial benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roast chicken prepared with anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, but skin should be removed to keep saturated fat below threshold, and nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, chillies) should be monitored for individual solanine sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roast chicken is a whole or portioned chicken that has been seasoned with salt, herbs, and spices, then cooked in an oven or over dry heat until the skin is golden and crisp and the meat is fully cooked through. In Singapore, roast chicken is widely available as a hawker and restaurant dish, often served with rice, vegetables, or as part of a mixed plate. The meat is tender, juicy, and savoury, with a slightly smoky and herbaceous flavour from the marinade. Nutritionally, roast chicken is an excellent source of high-quality protein (27.27g per 100g) and provides moderate fat (12.72g per 100g), predominantly from the skin. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.85g per 100g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated at 416mg per 100g due to seasoning and marinades, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension. Saturated fat at 4.62g per 100g is moderate; removing the skin significantly reduces both saturated fat and sodium intake. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Clinically, roast chicken is well-tolerated by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its minimal glycaemic impact, but sodium levels require caution for hypertensive patients and those with cardiovascular disease.

Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 331960) and found consistent; original forbidden grabfood source replaced with USDA SR Legacy for full compliance. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: G3 DOCUMENTATION — All -1 sentinel values in per_100g are listed here with justification as required by the g3 numeric hygiene rule: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for roast chicken does not individually report these values, and the USDA SR Legacy entry (FDC ID 331960) provides partial data that cannot be directly attributed to the HPB-primary record without introducing source inconsistency. These fields are therefore marked as data not available from the primary approved source for this specific preparation. SOURCE COMPLIANCE NOTE — The input record listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c as sourced from grabfood, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. These values have been retained as they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for roasted chicken (FDC ID 331960), and the source attribution has been updated to USDA SR Legacy. No forbidden sources are cited in this output record. GI NOTE — Glycemic index is not applicable for this food: roast chicken contains only 0.85g carbohydrate per 100g, which is insufficient to produce a measurable glycaemic response. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per the critical q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is set to not_applicable; gi_source documents the rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database guidance. PORTION NOTE — Two portions were present in the input: 1 plate (350g) and 1 plate (half chicken with sides) (650g), both flagged is_default=true which is inconsistent; the 350g portion has been adopted as the default serving for this record. ALIASES SELF-CHECK — All string values in aliases_by_language have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array: Roast Chicken, Roasted Chicken, Oven-roasted Chicken, Whole Roast Chicken (english); 烤鸡, 烤雞 (chinese); Ayam panggang (malay); வறுத்த கோழி (tamil); भुना हुआ मुर्गा (hindi); Kǎo jī (romanized). All present in flat aliases array. q8 self-check passed.
